summary.observation: Strong foundational addition skills. Errors suggest fatigue or number confusion rather than conceptual gaps. Skipped problem may indicate processing time needed.

notes_by_day.wednesday: Fire drill - sensory overload; lower count expected

behaviors_tracked.notes: Wednesday spike due to fire drill disruption; returned to baseline by Friday

maya_behavior_tally.overall_trend: Positive across all behaviors; greetings and directions improving, calming strategies showing good use

summary.observation: Jaylen successfully completed majority of routine. Pending steps at end of checklist suggest fatigue or task saturation. Monitor if clean-up is consistently skipped.

sofia_writing_sample.text: George Washington was the first president. He lived in Virginia. The Constitution was creaded in 1787. Washinton was an army leader before he was president.

sentences: George Washington was the first president.
Washinton was an army leader before he was president.

sofia_writing_sample.error_summary: 2 spelling errors detected. Both are phonetically reasonable attempts. No capitalization or punctuation errors.

rubric_estimate.rationale: Content accurate and well-organized (strength). Spelling errors moderate impact. Critical gap: lacks any emotional language, opinion, or abstract thinking. Meets baseline but not grade-level expectations for voice/emotion.

maya_math_worksheet.input_source: Vision Reader output - math worksheet (7/10 correct)

goal_mapping.goal_description: Maya will follow two-step verbal directions without visual supports with 75% accuracy across 8 trials
goal_mapping.rationale: Math worksheet represents multi-step task execution: 'read problem, write answer.' Independent work task demands direction-following and sustained attention.

trial_data.measurement_alignment: Within G2 measurement window; worksheet performance maps to direction-following domain

secondary_goals.reason: Not applicable - greetings not observed in worksheet context
Low probability - no behavioral dysregulation observed during task

maya_math_worksheet.notes: Worksheet performance provides objective data point for G2. The 70% accuracy aligns well with recent trial history (6/8=75% on 4/3). One skipped problem and two errors suggest need for visual supports during multi-step tasks.

jaylen_pecs_log.input_source: Vision Reader output - PECS communication log (5 attempts)

goal_mapping.goal_description: Jaylen will request wants/needs using AAC device with 80% accuracy across 15 opportunities per day

primary_measurement.success_rate_with_notes: 4 out of 5 attempts successful if counting picture+gesture hybrid as assisted success; 2 out of 5 if requiring AAC-only independence

independence_analysis.note: Goal target is 80% accuracy (success on demand). AAC device trending upward; gesture-reliance still present but decreasing.

trial_mapping.recent_trial_history_context: From IEP data: 3/15=33%, 3/22=47%, 3/29=60%, 4/1=73%, 4/3=80%. This PECS log confirms 4/3 target achievement.

jaylen_pecs_log.coaching_notes: AAC device use is increasing rapidly. The gesture responses should be acknowledged but gently redirected to AAC-first approach. Current trajectory suggests device preference will increase further.
jaylen_pecs_log.recommendation: Continue Thomas sticker reinforcement. Recommend rotating/reducing gesture acceptance in next phase to push AAC-only independence toward 90%+

sofia_writing_sample.input_source: Vision Reader output - Sofia's paragraph about Presidents

goal_mapping.goal_description: Sofia will demonstrate ability to write creatively beyond facts with 4 out of 5 writing samples meeting grade-level rubric expectations for voice, emotion, and abstract thinking

trend_context.recent_breakthrough: 4/1 onward with personal narrative + reflective essay assignments
trend_context.current_performance: Moving toward target but still fact-heavy

sofia_writing_sample.coaching_recommendation: Sofia is ready for opinion-insertion and feeling-extraction prompts. Model: 'Washington lived in Virginia AND I think that was interesting because [OPINION].' Use scaffold: 'What does this make you think/feel?'
sofia_writing_sample.note: Sample represents transition point - beginning to emerge from purely factual mode but not yet at target 80% (4/4 samples with rubric score 3+). Continue current instructional approach.

linear_regression.interpretation: Strong linear improvement; nearly perfect fit suggests consistent, measurable progress

data_quality.note: Clean, consistent upward trajectory with minimal noise

intervention_effectiveness.intervention: Peer buddy program + dinosaur reinforcer stickers + visual schedule of greeting scenarios
intervention_effectiveness.note: All three components appear to contribute to success. Peer buddy provides relationship context; stickers provide immediate reinforcement; visual schedule provides structure.

recommendations: Continue peer buddy program - highly effective
Begin fading sticker reinforcement frequency (shift from every-day to 2x/week) to build intrinsic motivation
Generalize greeting skills to new peers/settings (specials teachers, lunch aide)

plateau_detected.plateau_reason: New peer buddy; distraction from routine. Added verbal cueing on 3/29.

breakthrough_detected.breakthrough_intervention: Visual checklist added + verbal cueing ('listen with your whole body')

linear_regression.note: Moderate fit due to plateau. But recent 5-day trend (3/29-4/3) shows slope of ~6.25% per day.

maya_g2.confidence_note: Plateau followed by breakthrough. Target met, but continued monitoring recommended. If performance dips below 70% next week, consider intervention reinforcement.

intervention_analysis.initial_intervention: Visual schedule (was helping but not sufficient)

recommendations: Generalize visual checklist to novel contexts: specials classes, transitions, lunch period
Use peer buddy to model two-step directions (peer demonstrates, Maya follows)
Monitor for week 2-3 to ensure improvement is maintained; plateau risk remains

trend_analysis.interpretation: Consistent 12-15% weekly improvement suggests sustainable learning curve, not fluky gains

linear_regression.interpretation: Almost perfect linear fit. Jaylen is learning AAC device use at a very predictable, steady rate.

mastery_projection.projected_continuation: At current slope, Jaylen could reach 90-95% by end of April

interventions: AAC device setup with pre-programmed phrases
Thomas the Tank Engine sticker reinforcement
intervention_effectiveness.most_effective: Thomas sticker reinforcement + speech therapy + AAC device design

recommendations: Continue Thomas sticker motivation - working excellently
Expand AAC device phrase library to include more complex requests (multi-word combinations)
Monitor for gesture regression; gently redirect to AAC-first when gestures emerge
Plan fading of sticker reinforcement starting late April

plateau_detected.plateau_characterization: Long, resistant plateau. Sofia was stuck in fact-only mode despite coaching.

breakthrough_detected.breakthrough_intervention: Shift to personal narrative assignments + reflective essay prompts

linear_regression.note: Moderate fit overall due to plateau. But slope post-breakthrough (4/1-4/3) would be ~20%/day.

sofia_g3.alert_reason: ESCALATION RECOMMENDED - Long plateau (14 days) before breakthrough is concerning. If performance reverts below 75%, immediate intervention change required.
sofia_g3.confidence_note: Just met target, but trajectory suggests fragility. Plateau was long and resistant. Breakthrough only occurred with significant instructional shift.

intervention_analysis.initial_intervention: Social skills coaching + writing coach assigned

critical_intervention.change_magnitude: Major instructional pivot required, not just coaching

recommendations: CONTINUE CURRENT APPROACH - Personal narrative + reflection is working. This is not the time for change.
MONITOR CLOSELY - If performance drops below 75% in next 2 data points, escalate immediately to:
- 1:1 writing coaching with focus on emotion/opinion language
PLAN PROACTIVELY - When establishing new goals, use 'front-load' instructional design rather than waiting for plateau
sofia_g3.risk_flag: Sofia's learning profile suggests she benefits from explicit, scaffolded instruction when transitioning to abstract thinking. Monitor for similar plateaus in future goals.

lesson_plan_maya_g1.title: Maya's Dinosaur Greeting Adventures
lesson_plan_maya_g1.current_performance: 80% - TARGET MET. Plan focuses on maintenance and generalization.

activities.title: Blue the Raptor Greeting Game
activities.description: Daily warm-up (5 minutes). Maya approaches a Blue raptor toy during morning arrival. She practices greeting the toy in different ways: wave, 'hi Blue', dinosaur roar + gesture, etc.
materials: Blue raptor plush toy or figure
activities.implementation: Teacher models greeting first day. By day 3-4, Maya leads. Practice across different times of day.
activities.reinforcer: 2 dinosaur stickers per successful greeting interaction

activities.title: Dinosaur Handshake
activities.description: Peer greeting variation. Two students (Maya + peer buddy) perform a special 'dinosaur handshake' before transitioning: high-five, low-five, roar together.
materials: Visual diagram of handshake steps (3 steps with pictures)
activities.implementation: Introduce with familiar peer buddy first. Fade to introducing to new peers.
activities.settings: Morning arrival, lunch, specials transitions, free play

activities.title: Dino Sticker Challenge - 7-Day Greeting Goal
activities.description: Visual tracker. Maya earns 1 sticker per successful greeting across 3 different settings per day. After 7 stickers, choose reinforcer (5 min water table OR iPad counting app).
activities.measurement: Teacher tally across morning (arrival), lunch, specials, and free play = 4 daily opportunities

reinforcement_schedule.delayed: After 7 stickers: 5 min water table or app time
reinforcement_schedule.fading_plan: Week 1-2: continuous stickers. Week 3-4: intermittent (every 2nd greeting). Ongoing: verbal praise + high-fives

supports: Weighted lap pad during wait times

lesson_plan_maya_g1.data_collection: Teacher tally of successful greetings per setting; weekly graph of % success
lesson_plan_maya_g1.generalization_plan: Expand from peer buddy + familiar peers → unfamiliar peers → specials teachers → lunch aide (by week 2-3)
lesson_plan_maya_g1.notes: Maya has met target (80%). This lesson plan maintains achievement and generalizes to new settings. Fading stickers to build intrinsic motivation.

social_story_maya_transitions.title: I Am Brave Like Blue the Raptor
social_story_maya_transitions.format: Carol Gray (first person, 2:1 ratio: descriptive:coaching sentences)
social_story_maya_transitions.text:
My name is Maya.
I like dinosaurs.
Blue the raptor is brave.
Blue knows how to move from one place to another.

Transitions mean we move from one activity to another.
Everyone in our class has transitions.
I can be brave during transitions like Blue.

Sometimes transitions feel bumpy or loud.
My body might feel worried.
I can use my noise-canceling headphones to help.

I have special tools to help me.
I have a fidget cube.
I have a weighted lap pad.
I can go to the water table and play.
These tools help me feel calm like Blue feels safe in her den.

I am learning to be brave during transitions.
I am like Blue the raptor.

structure.opening: Self-identification + character interest + definition of transitions
structure.closing: Affirmation of identity + parallel to Blue

visuals: 3 coping strategy boxes: headphones icon, fidget cube, weighted pad, water table
social_story_maya_transitions.implementation: Read together each morning. Teacher points to visuals. Keep printed copy in Maya's sensory kit for reference before transitions.
social_story_maya_transitions.reinforcement: After successful use of strategy during transition, earn dinosaur sticker
social_story_maya_transitions.notes: Story normalizes transitions, acknowledges sensory reality, and provides concrete coping tools. Blue serves as brave role model (Maya's special interest).

goal_1.description: Maya will initiate or respond to peer greetings (verbal or gesture-based) with 80% accuracy across 10 trials in a variety of settings
goal_1.measurement_method: Teacher observation tally across 4 settings: arrival, lunch, specials, free play
goal_1.intervention: Peer buddy program + dinosaur sticker reinforcement + visual greeting scenario cards
goal_1.intervention_effectiveness: Excellent - clear correlation between peer buddy introduction (3/15) and immediate improvement trajectory
goal_1.observation_notes: Maya now initiates greetings with familiar peers and responds consistently to peer initiations. Still somewhat withdrawn in large groups but one-on-one greeting skills solidified. Peer buddy program highly effective; stickers are strong reinforcer.
goal_1.recommendation: Continue peer buddy program. Begin fading sticker frequency to promote intrinsic motivation (shift to 2x/week by end of April). Introduce generalization to unfamiliar peers and specials teachers.

goal_2.description: Maya will follow two-step verbal directions without visual supports with 75% accuracy across 8 trials during independent work, routines, and transitions
goal_2.measurement_method: Teacher checklist across 8 trials during independent work time and transitions
goal_2.trend: Plateau 3/15-3/29, improvement 3/29 onward with visual checklist + verbal cueing intervention
goal_2.intervention: Added verbal cue 'Listen with your whole body' + visual checklist of two-step tasks paired with verbal direction
goal_2.intervention_effectiveness: High - immediate improvement correlates with intervention introduction
goal_2.observation_notes: Maya benefits significantly from visual support (checklist) paired with verbal direction. Still struggles with novel/unpracticed two-step tasks but excels with routine directions (routines, transitions). Requires brief processing time before task initiation.
goal_2.recommendation: Generalize visual checklist approach to specials classes, lunch, and transitions. Introduce 'checklist buddy' system where peer helps Maya access visual support. Monitor for continued 70%+ performance; if dips below, reinforce visual checklist protocol.

goal_3.description: Maya will reduce the frequency and duration of outbursts to an average of 1 or fewer per school day, with duration under 5 minutes
goal_3.measurement_method: Behavior log (frequency per day, duration in minutes) tracked across 5-day weeks

trial_data.april_3: 1.0 outburst/week (100% - 6 outbursts last week, down from 12-13 previously)

goal_3.trend: Steady decline in frequency; durations consistently under 5 minutes by week of 3/29
goal_3.intervention: Sensory support package: weighted lap pad (proprioceptive), noise-canceling headphones (auditory protection), fidget cube (fine motor regulation)
goal_3.intervention_effectiveness: Excellent - clear correlation between sensory supports introduction (around 3/29) and dramatic frequency reduction
goal_3.observation_notes: Outbursts were most commonly triggered by: loud transitions, fluorescent lighting in art room, unexpected schedule changes. With sensory supports in place, triggers are manageable. Outbursts when they occur are brief (2-4 min) and Maya recovers well with fidget cube + quiet space. Fire drill (3/29) caused 2 major meltdowns but was manageable with weighted vest + headphones.
goal_3.risk_note: One concerning outburst early April related to inability to access water table during scheduled time - suggests external control/access is still important for regulation. Monitor.
goal_3.recommendation: Continue sensory support package. Consider fading one support element at a time in May to assess true maintenance (vs. external crutch). Ensure water table access remains available as preferred coping mechanism. Introduce breathing exercises as complementary self-regulation tool for use alongside sensory supports.

admin_report_maya.summary_statement: Maya has made significant progress across all three IEP goals, meeting targets in Communication, Following Directions, and Self-Regulation over a 3-week period. Her success reflects a combination of effective peer relationship building (peer buddy program), consistent visual support strategies (checklists, schedules), and proactive sensory accommodations (headphones, weighted pad, fidget tools). Maya demonstrates emerging self-awareness of her sensory needs and can now request sensory breaks when needed. Her communication improvements are most evident in structured, familiar contexts; continued generalization to novel settings and unfamiliar peers is recommended.
next_steps: Schedule IEP review meeting to celebrate progress and set new goals for next school year
Plan sensory support fading protocol (beginning May) to assess durability of learned skills
Introduce generalization activities for peer greetings across specials classes
Document effective interventions (peer buddy, visual checklist, sensory kit) for transition to next teacher
admin_report_maya.overall_rating: Excellent progress. All goals met. Intervention strategies evidence-based and effective. Maya is on track for strong end-of-year outcomes.

parent_communication_maya.greeting:
Hi [Parent Name],

Maya had another great day!

body.section_1_math: Today Maya completed a math worksheet with 7 out of 10 correct (70%). This is showing she's getting better at following multi-step directions! The problems she missed were the harder ones (4+4, 3+2), and she skipped one problem, which might mean she was working fast or needed a little break.
body.section_2_progress:
I also wanted to celebrate some big wins this week:

• Greetings with Classmates: Maya is now greeting her classmates 80% of the time — that was her goal! We met it! She's especially confident with her peer buddy and is starting to say hi to other students too.

• Following Directions: Maya is doing really well following two-step directions (like 'Get your pencil and sit down'). She's at 75% — her target!

• Staying Calm: The weighted lap pad and noise-canceling headphones are making a big difference. Maya is having fewer big feelings and bouncing back faster when transitions are hard.
body.section_3_home_activity:
Here's something fun to try at home:

Practice two-step directions during play time. For example: 'Get your dinosaur and put it in the purple box.' Or 'Find the number 5 and show me.' Maya loves dinosaurs and counting, so this might be a great way to practice AND have fun together!
body.section_4_sensory_note: The sensory tools we talked about are really helping Maya feel better during the day. When you pick her up, you might notice she's calmer — that's the weighted lap pad and fidget cube working! Let's keep those strategies going at home too.
body.section_5_closing: Maya is working so hard, and we're celebrating her progress every single day. Keep up the great work!

parent_communication_maya.visual_elements: Small dinosaur clip-art in corner; simple layout; approximately half-page (fits in backpack folder)

parent_communication_jaylen.greeting:
Hi [Parent Name],

Big celebration this week!

body.section_1_aac_win: Jaylen met his communication goal! He is now using his AAC device to ask for what he wants 80% of the time. This is HUGE! It means Jaylen is communicating his needs independently.
body.section_2_specific_examples:
This week, Jaylen asked for:
• Snack time (using AAC)
• A sensory break (using AAC + picture card)
• The Thomas trains (using AAC device)

These are all things Jaylen used to ask for with gestures or pointing. Now he's using words (via his device) — that's real communication!
body.section_3_thomas_reinforcer: The Thomas the Tank Engine stickers are super motivating for Jaylen. Every time he uses his AAC, his eyes light up when he earns a sticker. We're collecting them for him to trade for Thomas train time. It's working beautifully!
body.section_4_home_practice:
At home, you can practice the same way:
• Give Jaylen choices: 'Do you want snack or drink?' Let him point to his AAC button.
• Wait quietly for him to respond (give him a few seconds).
• Celebrate when he communicates — big smiles, thumbs up, stickers!
• Gently encourage AAC over gestures ('Show me with your device first, then we can sign').
body.section_5_schedule_note: Jaylen is also doing great with his visual schedule! He's following it almost by himself, which means he's becoming more independent every day.
body.section_6_closing: Jaylen is an amazing communicator. We're so proud of his hard work! Keep celebrating his AAC use — it's building his confidence every day.

parent_communication_jaylen.visual_elements: Train clip-art (Thomas-style); enthusiastic tone throughout; short paragraphs; approximately half-page
